topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and unwelcome, on my phone screen. A picture of me, taken at the school's annual science fair, was shared by Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet library. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to focus on the textbook in front of me. He’d tagged me in a post about my project, a casual acknowledgment that now felt like an invasion. I kept glancing at the screen, a knot tightening in my stomach. Every time I looked up, I imagined his eyes tracking my movements, cataloging the moments I thought were private. I shoved my phone deep into my bag, the fabric rough against my palm, trying to will the unsettling feeling away.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at my reflection in the darkened computer monitor, my face pale under the glow of the screen. It was the profile picture I saw—a slightly blurred shot of me from a class presentation. Mr. Harrison had liked it. The warmth of the interaction felt suffocating. I started fiddling with the edge of my desk, a nervous habit I hadn't possessed before. Why did he need to see this? It felt like a spotlight had been unexpectedly turned onto my life, exposing things I hadn't intended to share. I reread the caption of the post, trying to find some justification for the shared image, but the words blurred. A flush crept up my neck, and I quickly turned away, focusing intently on the complex equations on my worksheet, hoping the silence would swallow the sudden, heavy awareness.

[story 3]

It was late, and I was scrolling aimlessly through Instagram when I saw it. A photo from a recent tutoring session, taken by Mr. Harrison. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. A wave of heat washed over my cheeks, making me want to sink into my chair. I felt a distinct urge to delete the application entirely, to erase the digital footprint of that moment. I spent the next ten minutes compulsively checking my privacy settings, a frantic energy buzzing beneath my skin. When I finally closed the app, the silence of my room felt amplified, pressing in on me. I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to swim on the page. I couldn't seem to concentrate on the assignment; my attention kept drifting back to the unseen audience of that shared image.
